aliases: Indian vegetable fritters in chickpea batter
food_subgroup: Deep-fried spiced vegetable fritters in chickpea flour batter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Vegetable Pakora. Nearest parent is a deep-fried vegetable snack. A new concept for Indian pakora-style fritters is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried mixed vegetable fritters in legume batter (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of vegetable pakora is 6 pieces (approximately 150g total) — crispy, golden-brown irregular fritters of mixed vegetables coated in a spiced chickpea flour batter, served with mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Pakora — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from besan-battered deep-fried vegetable data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone vegetable pakora entry. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN chickpea flour (besan) composition data and standard deep-frying oil absorption (approximately 18–20g oil per 100g cooked pakora) with mixed vegetable proportion (~40% of batter weight).

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from chickpea-battered deep-fried vegetable; chickpea flour has a low GI (~28) which significantly moderates the overall GI despite deep-frying.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 428mg per 6-piece serving (150g) — moderate; salt in batter and mint chutney dipping sauce contribute to sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Suitable in moderation for hypertensive patients. The high potassium from vegetables and chickpea flour (428mg per serving) is beneficial. Limit the mint chutney which may be high in salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~38) due to chickpea flour base; glycaemic load per 6-piece serving is approximately 18 — moderate. The chickpea flour provides excellent glycaemic control despite deep-frying.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetable pakora is an acceptable occasional snack for diabetic patients — the low-GI chickpea flour batter is beneficial. Limit to 3–4 pieces. Avoid eating with high-sugar chutneys.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying adds omega-6 polyunsaturated fats which in excess can promote inflammation; however, ajwain (carom seeds) and turmeric in the batter are anti-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Consume occasionally. The chickpea flour provides excellent folate and protein, and the spices provide anti-inflammatory benefits, but the deep-frying method means regular consumption is not recommended for patients with active arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Pakora is a classic Indian snack — sliced or diced mixed vegetables (onion, potato, spinach, green chilli, cauliflower) coated in a thick, spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter seasoned with ajwain (carom seeds), red chilli, and coriander, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The chickpea flour batter creates a distinctive, slightly nutty and crispy coating. Pakoras are universally available at Indian restaurants in Singapore as a starter or snack. Despite the deep-frying, the chickpea flour base gives pakoras a surprisingly low glycaemic index.

confidence_reason: No standalone vegetable pakora entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN chickpea flour (besan) and mixed vegetable data with standard deep-frying oil absorption. Chickpea flour composition is well-established.
notes: Contains legume allergen (chickpea flour). Vegan and gluten-free in standard preparation. Cholesterol is 0.0. The oil quality used for frying significantly affects the health profile — fresh oil vs reused oil changes the trans fat and polar compound content. High caloric density from oil absorption (~17.5g fat/100g).
